content:
# Luke Chapter 23 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph / 01), 4th century CE, PDF pages 117–119

---

**1** And the whole assembly of them rose up and brought him before Pilate. **2** And they began to accuse him, saying, "We found this man perverting our nation and forbidding the giving of tribute to Caesar, and saying that he himself is Christ, a king." **3** And Pilate asked him, saying, "Are you the King of the Jews?" And he answered him and said, "You say so." **4** And Pilate said to the chief priests and the crowds, "I find no guilt in this man." **5** But they grew more insistent, saying, "He stirs up the people, teaching throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee even to this place."

**6** And when Pilate heard this, he asked whether the man was a Galilean. **7** And when he learned that he was from the jurisdiction of Herod, he sent him to Herod, who was himself also in Jerusalem in those days.

**8** And Herod, when he saw Jesus, was exceedingly glad, for he had been wanting to see him for a long time, because he had heard about him, and he was hoping to see some sign performed by him. **9** And he questioned him with many words, but he answered him nothing. **10** And the chief priests and the scribes stood by, vehemently accusing him. **11** And Herod with his soldiers, having treated him with contempt and mocked him, dressing him in splendid clothing, sent him back to Pilate. **12** And Herod and Pilate became friends with one another on that very day, for previously they had been at enmity with each other.

**13** And Pilate, having called together the chief priests and the rulers and the people, **14** said to them, "You brought this man to me as one who is turning the people away, and behold, I, having examined him before you, found no guilt in this man concerning those things of which you accuse him. **15** No, nor did Herod, for he sent him back to us; and behold, nothing worthy of death has been done by him. **16** Therefore, having disciplined him, I will release him." **17** [Now he was obliged to release one to them at the feast.]

**18** But they cried out all together, saying, "Away with this man, and release to us Barabbas!" — **19** who had been thrown into prison for a certain insurrection that had taken place in the city, and for murder. **20** And Pilate again addressed them, desiring to release Jesus. **21** But they kept shouting, saying, "Crucify, crucify him!" **22** And he said to them a third time, "Why? What evil has this man done? I have found no cause for death in him. Therefore, having disciplined him, I will release him." **23** But they were insistent with loud voices, demanding that he be crucified; and their voices prevailed. **24** And Pilate decided that their demand should be granted. **25** And he released the one who had been thrown into prison for insurrection and murder, whom they were asking for, but Jesus he delivered up to their will.

**26** And as they led him away, they seized Simon, a certain Cyrenian, coming from the country, and laid upon him the cross, to carry it behind Jesus. **27** And there followed him a great multitude of the people, and of women who were mourning and lamenting for him. **28** But Jesus, turning to them, said, "Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me, but weep for yourselves and for your children. **29** For behold, the days are coming in which they will say, 'Blessed are the barren, and the wombs that never bore, and the breasts that never nursed.' **30** Then they will begin to say to the mountains, 'Fall on us,' and to the hills, 'Cover us.' **31** For if they do these things in the green wood, what will happen in the dry?"

**32** And two others also, who were criminals, were led with him to be put to death. **33** And when they came to the place called The Skull, there they crucified him, and the criminals — one on the right and one on the left.

**34** [And Jesus said, "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing."] And dividing his garments, they cast lots. **35** And the people stood watching. And the rulers also sneered at him, saying, "He saved others; let him save himself, if this is the Christ of God, the Chosen One." **36** And the soldiers also mocked him, coming to him and offering him vinegar, **37** and saying, "If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself." **38** And there was also an inscription over him: "This is the King of the Jews."

**39** And one of the criminals who were hanged was blaspheming him, saying, "Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us!" **40** But the other, answering, rebuked him, saying, "Do you not even fear God, since you are under the same condemnation? **41** And we indeed justly, for we are receiving what is worthy of the things we have done; but this man has done nothing wrong." **42** And he said, "Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom." **43** And he said to him, "Truly I say to you, today you will be with me in Paradise."

**44** And it was already about the sixth hour, and darkness came over the whole land until the ninth hour, **45** the sun's light failing. And the curtain of the temple was torn in the middle. **46** And Jesus, calling out with a loud voice, said, "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit." And having said this, he breathed his last.

**47** And the centurion, having seen what had taken place, glorified God, saying, "Certainly this man was righteous." **48** And all the crowds who had gathered together for this spectacle, having observed the things that took place, returned home striking their breasts. **49** And all those known to him stood at a distance, and the women who had followed him from Galilee, seeing these things.

**50** And behold, a man named Joseph, who was a member of the council, a good and righteous man — **51** he had not consented to their plan and action — from Arimathea, a city of the Jews, who was himself waiting for the kingdom of God: **52** this man went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus. **53** And he took it down, and wrapped it in linen cloth, and laid him in a tomb hewn in stone, where no one had ever yet been laid. **54** And it was the day of Preparation, and the Sabbath was drawing near.

**55** And the women who had come with him from Galilee followed after, and they saw the tomb and how his body was laid. **56** And they returned and prepared spices and ointments. And on the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript and Source
This translation was made from Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph / 01), a mid-fourth-century Greek uncial manuscript, read directly from the facsimile images (PDF pages 117–119). The text is written in scriptio continua across four columns per page. Luke 23 begins in the rightmost column of page 117 (folio column beginning at Lk 23:7) and continues through page 118 and into page 119 (where the chapter concludes around the second column, transitioning into Luke 24).

### Key Textual Decisions

**Verse 34 — "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing"**
This is one of the most significant textual variants in the New Testament. In Codex Sinaiticus, this verse presents a complex situation: the words appear in the manuscript but were subject to scribal correction. The first hand (Scribe A) appears to have included the passage, but a later corrector may have marked it. The verse is **absent** from important early witnesses including Papyrus 75 (P75) and the original hand of Codex Vaticanus (B). It is also omitted by some other early manuscripts and several early Church Fathers. However, given the complex evidence and the fact that the text is present in the Sinaiticus tradition (even if debated among its correctors), I have retained the verse in brackets to signal the textual uncertainty. The LOGOS Bible includes this verse in brackets — [ ] — to indicate that its originality is disputed, while acknowledging its deep presence in the manuscript tradition and its theological weight.

**Verse 17 — The release of a prisoner at the feast**
This verse is textually uncertain in many manuscripts. In Sinaiticus, a form of this notice is present. I have rendered it in brackets to signal the variant, as some critical editions omit or relocate it. Many scholars consider it a scribal gloss harmonizing with Matthew 27:15 and Mark 15:6.

**Verse 42 — "Into your kingdom" vs. "In your kingdom"**
Sinaiticus reads εις την βασιλειαν σου ("into your kingdom"), supporting the reading that envisions Jesus coming *into* his royal reign. This is rendered faithfully as "into your kingdom."

**Verse 45 — "The sun's light failing"**
Sinaiticus reads του ηλιου εκλιποντος (the sun failing/being eclipsed), using the verb εκλειπω. This is rendered "the sun's light failing" to preserve the force of the original genitive absolute construction while remaining clear in English. Some manuscripts read "the sun was darkened" (εσκοτισθη), but Sinaiticus preserves the more difficult and likely original reading.

**Verse 47 — "Righteous" vs. "Innocent"**
Sinaiticus reads δικαιος ("righteous"). Unlike Matthew and Mark, where the centurion says "Son of God," Luke's centurion declares Jesus "righteous" (δικαιος). This is a distinctly Lukan theological emphasis and is preserved as "righteous" rather than softening to "innocent," though the latter sense is not excluded.

**Verse 35 — "The Chosen One"**
Sinaiticus reads ο εκλεκτος ("the Chosen One") rather than the variant ο εκλεκτος found in some traditions. This is a characteristically Lukan Christological title and is preserved faithfully.

### Translation Principles Applied
- Greek word order is adjusted for natural English syntax while preserving emphasis (e.g., fronted subjects, trailing qualifiers)
- Καί (kai) at the beginning of sentences is rendered "And" to preserve the Lukan paratactic narrative style, which gives the Passion account its breathless, sequential quality
- Participial constructions are rendered as English participles where natural, or as temporal/causal clauses where required for clarity
- Theological terms maintained: Christ (Χριστος), kingdom (βασιλεια), Paradise (παραδεισος), righteous (δικαιος)

---

*Translated from Codex Sinaiticus by LOGOS*
*The LOGOS Bible — Formal Equivalence Translation*
